question: Can I deduct mortgage interest on my primary home?
ground_truth: Yes. You can deduct home mortgage interest on your main home if the loan is secured by your home and the total mortgage debt does not exceed $750,000 ($375,000 if married filing separately). See IRS Publication 936.

question: What is the standard deduction for a single filer in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, the standard deduction for a single filer is $14,600. For married filing jointly it is $29,200. Additional amounts apply if you are 65 or older or blind. See IRS Publication 501.

question: Are contributions to a traditional IRA tax deductible?
ground_truth: Contributions to a traditional IRA may be fully or partially deductible depending on whether you (or your spouse) are covered by a workplace retirement plan and your modified adjusted gross income (MAGI). If neither you nor your spouse is covered by a workplace plan, contributions are fully deductible. See IRS Publication 590-A.

question: How long must I hold a stock to qualify for long-term capital gains rates?
ground_truth: You must hold the asset for more than one year (more than 12 months) to qualify for the lower long-term capital gains tax rates. Assets held one year or less are subject to short-term capital gains rates, which are the same as ordinary income rates. See IRS Publication 550.

question: Is money received as a gift taxable income to the recipient?
ground_truth: No. Gifts are not taxable income to the recipient. The person who gives the gift may have gift tax obligations, but the recipient does not report the gift as income. Inheritances and bequests are also generally not included in gross income. See IRS Publication 525.

question: What filing status can a single parent use if they paid more than half the cost of keeping a home for a qualifying child?
ground_truth: A single parent who paid more than half the cost of maintaining a home for a qualifying child for more than half the year may file as Head of Household. This status provides a higher standard deduction than Single filing status and lower tax rates. See IRS Publication 17.

question: What is the penalty for filing a federal tax return late when tax is owed?
ground_truth: The failure-to-file penalty is generally 5% of the unpaid tax for each month or part of a month the return is late, up to a maximum of 25%. If the return is more than 60 days late, the minimum penalty is the smaller of $485 or 100% of the unpaid tax. See IRS Publication 17.

question: What is the gross income filing threshold for a single filer under age 65 in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, a single filer under age 65 must file a federal income tax return if gross income is at least $14,600. This equals the standard deduction for that filing status. You may still want to file even if below the threshold to receive a refund of withheld taxes or to claim refundable credits. See IRS Publication 17.

question: What is the additional standard deduction amount for a single taxpayer who is age 65 or older in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, a single taxpayer who is age 65 or older can claim an additional standard deduction of $1,950. This is added to the regular $14,600 standard deduction for a total of $16,550. A separate additional amount applies if the taxpayer is blind. See IRS Publication 501.

question: What is the gross income limit for claiming someone as a qualifying relative dependent?
ground_truth: To claim someone as a qualifying relative, the person's gross income must be less than $5,050 for 2024. There is no gross income limit for a qualifying child who is your child and either under age 19 or a full-time student under age 24. See IRS Publication 501.

question: What percentage of adjusted gross income must medical expenses exceed before they become deductible?
ground_truth: You can deduct only the portion of your medical and dental expenses that exceeds 7.5% of your adjusted gross income. For example, if your AGI is $50,000, only unreimbursed medical expenses above $3,750 are deductible. See IRS Publication 502.

question: Can I deduct the cost of prescription eyeglasses as a medical expense?
ground_truth: Yes. Prescription eyeglasses and contact lenses are deductible medical expenses, subject to the 7.5% of AGI threshold. Eye exams and the cost of laser eye surgery are also deductible. Non-prescription sunglasses and contact lenses worn for cosmetic reasons are not deductible. See IRS Publication 502.

question: Can I deduct health insurance premiums I pay out of pocket as a medical expense?
ground_truth: Yes. Health insurance premiums paid with after-tax dollars are deductible as medical expenses, including premiums for medical, dental, and qualifying long-term care coverage. Premiums paid on a pre-tax basis through an employer plan are already excluded from income and cannot be deducted again. The deduction is subject to the 7.5% AGI threshold. See IRS Publication 502.

question: What is the maximum amount of qualifying expenses for the child and dependent care credit for one qualifying person?
ground_truth: The maximum qualifying expenses for the child and dependent care credit are $3,000 for one qualifying person and $6,000 for two or more qualifying persons. The credit is a percentage of these expenses ranging from 20% to 35% depending on adjusted gross income. See IRS Publication 503.

question: What is the maximum age of a child who qualifies for the child and dependent care credit?
ground_truth: A qualifying child for the child and dependent care credit must be under age 13 at the time the care is provided. There is no age limit if the qualifying person is your spouse or a dependent who is physically or mentally incapable of self-care. See IRS Publication 503.

question: Can I claim the child and dependent care credit if I pay a relative to care for my child?
ground_truth: You may pay a relative for child care and claim the credit, but the care provider cannot be your spouse, the child's parent, your dependent, or your own child who is under age 19. Payments to an ineligible provider do not qualify. See IRS Publication 503.

question: How does an employer-provided dependent care assistance program affect the child and dependent care credit?
ground_truth: Amounts excluded from your income through an employer-sponsored dependent care assistance program or flexible spending account reduce the qualifying expenses you can use for the child and dependent care credit. You subtract employer-provided benefits from the maximum qualifying expense limit before calculating the credit. See IRS Publication 503.

question: What is the safe harbor rule for avoiding the underpayment penalty on estimated taxes?
ground_truth: To avoid the underpayment penalty, total payments during the year must equal the smaller of 90% of the tax shown on the current year return or 100% of the tax shown on the prior year return. If your prior year adjusted gross income exceeded $150,000, the prior-year safe harbor rises to 110%. See IRS Publication 505.

question: When are quarterly estimated tax payments due?
ground_truth: For most individual taxpayers, quarterly estimated tax payments are due on April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 of the following year. If the due date falls on a weekend or legal holiday, the deadline moves to the next business day. See IRS Publication 505.

question: How much tax must I expect to owe before I am required to make estimated tax payments?
ground_truth: You generally must make estimated tax payments if you expect to owe at least $1,000 in federal income tax after subtracting withholding and refundable credits, and your withholding will cover less than 90% of your current year tax liability or 100% of last year's tax. See IRS Publication 505.

question: What is the maximum gain a single taxpayer can exclude from income when selling a main home?
ground_truth: A single taxpayer can exclude up to $250,000 of gain from the sale of a main home. Married taxpayers filing jointly can exclude up to $500,000 if both spouses meet the use test and neither spouse has used the exclusion in the two years before the sale. See IRS Publication 523.

question: How long must I have owned and lived in my home to qualify for the home sale gain exclusion?
ground_truth: To qualify for the home sale exclusion, you must have owned and used the property as your main home for at least two out of the five years ending on the date of sale. The two years of ownership and two years of use do not need to be the same two-year period. See IRS Publication 523.

question: Do I need to report the sale of my main home on my tax return if my gain is below the exclusion limit?
ground_truth: You generally do not need to report the sale on your tax return if the gain is fully excluded and you did not receive a Form 1099-S. If you received a Form 1099-S or the gain exceeds the exclusion, you must report the transaction on Schedule D. See IRS Publication 523.

question: Can I deduct a loss from selling my main home?
ground_truth: No. A loss on the sale of your main home is not deductible. Losses from selling personal-use property are not deductible. This is different from investment or rental property, where losses may be deductible subject to other limitations. See IRS Publication 523.

question: Is unemployment compensation taxable income?
ground_truth: Yes. Unemployment compensation is fully taxable and must be included in your gross income. You will generally receive Form 1099-G reporting the amount received. You can elect to have federal income tax withheld from unemployment benefits by submitting Form W-4V to the paying agency. See IRS Publication 525.

question: Are workers' compensation benefits taxable?
ground_truth: No. Workers' compensation benefits received for a work-related injury or illness under a workers' compensation act are generally not taxable and are excluded from gross income. See IRS Publication 525.

question: What is the AGI limit for deducting cash contributions to public charities?
ground_truth: Cash contributions to public charities, certain private foundations, and governmental units are deductible up to 60% of your adjusted gross income. Contributions exceeding this limit may be carried forward for up to five years. See IRS Publication 526.

question: What documentation is required for a charitable cash donation of $250 or more?
ground_truth: For a single cash donation of $250 or more, you must obtain a contemporaneous written acknowledgment from the charity that states the amount contributed, whether any goods or services were provided in return, and a description and good-faith estimate of the value of any such consideration. A bank record alone does not meet this requirement. See IRS Publication 526.

question: Can I deduct the value of time I spend volunteering for a charitable organization?
ground_truth: No. The value of your time or services donated to a charity is not deductible. However, unreimbursed out-of-pocket expenses you incur while performing volunteer services, such as transportation, may be deductible. The standard mileage rate for charitable driving is 14 cents per mile. See IRS Publication 526.

question: What form is required for noncash charitable contributions worth more than $500?
ground_truth: If your total noncash charitable contributions exceed $500, you must complete Form 8283 and attach it to your return. For contributions of property valued over $5,000 (other than publicly traded securities), you generally need a qualified appraisal conducted by a qualified appraiser. See IRS Publication 526.

question: What is the wash sale rule?
ground_truth: The wash sale rule disallows a loss on the sale of stock or securities if you purchase substantially identical stock or securities within 30 days before or after the sale. The disallowed loss is not gone permanently; it is added to the cost basis of the replacement shares. See IRS Publication 550.

question: How are qualified dividends taxed compared to ordinary dividends?
ground_truth: Qualified dividends are taxed at the preferential long-term capital gains rates of 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on your taxable income. Ordinary dividends are taxed as ordinary income at your regular marginal tax rate. To be qualified, dividends must be paid by a U.S. corporation or qualifying foreign corporation on stock held for more than 60 days during the required holding period. See IRS Publication 550.

question: Can I deduct investment interest expense on my tax return?
ground_truth: Yes. Investment interest expense, which is interest paid on money you borrowed to buy taxable investment property, is deductible. The deduction is limited to your net investment income for the year. Any excess can be carried forward to future tax years. See IRS Publication 550.

question: What is the annual IRA contribution limit for 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, the annual limit for contributions to all your traditional and Roth IRAs combined is $7,000. If you are age 50 or older by the end of the year, you can make an additional $1,000 catch-up contribution for a total of $8,000. See IRS Publication 590-A.

question: What is the modified AGI phase-out range for contributing to a Roth IRA as a single filer in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, single filers can make a full Roth IRA contribution if their modified adjusted gross income is below $146,000. The allowable contribution phases out between $146,000 and $161,000. Above $161,000 MAGI, no Roth IRA contribution is permitted. See IRS Publication 590-A.

question: Can I contribute to both a traditional IRA and a Roth IRA in the same tax year?
ground_truth: Yes. You may contribute to both a traditional IRA and a Roth IRA in the same year, but your total combined contributions to all IRAs cannot exceed $7,000 ($8,000 if age 50 or older). Each account type has its own eligibility and deductibility rules that apply separately. See IRS Publication 590-A.

question: At what age must I begin taking required minimum distributions from a traditional IRA?
ground_truth: Under the SECURE 2.0 Act, you must begin taking required minimum distributions from your traditional IRA by April 1 of the year following the year you turn age 73. Roth IRAs are not subject to required minimum distributions during the owner's lifetime. See IRS Publication 590-B.

question: What is the additional tax penalty for taking an early distribution from a traditional IRA before age 59.5?
ground_truth: If you take a distribution from your traditional IRA before age 59.5, you generally owe an additional 10% early distribution tax on the taxable amount, on top of regular income tax. Exceptions include distributions due to disability, certain unreimbursed medical expenses, first-time home purchase up to $10,000, and qualified higher education expenses. See IRS Publication 590-B.

question: What is the 60-day rollover rule for IRA distributions?
ground_truth: If you receive a distribution from an IRA, you can avoid tax by rolling the funds into another IRA or eligible retirement plan within 60 days of receipt. You are permitted only one IRA-to-IRA rollover per 12-month period across all your IRAs. Missing the 60-day deadline generally makes the distribution fully taxable. See IRS Publication 590-B.

question: Are qualified distributions from a Roth IRA tax-free?
ground_truth: Yes. A qualified distribution from a Roth IRA is tax-free and not subject to the 10% early withdrawal tax. A distribution is qualified if the Roth IRA has been open for at least five years and you are age 59.5 or older, disabled, deceased, or withdrawing up to $10,000 for a first-time home purchase. Distributions that do not meet these requirements may be partly taxable. See IRS Publication 590-B.

question: What is the maximum earned income credit for a taxpayer with three or more qualifying children in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, the maximum earned income credit for a taxpayer with three or more qualifying children is $7,830. The maximum credit is $6,960 for two qualifying children, $4,213 for one qualifying child, and $632 for taxpayers with no qualifying children. See IRS Publication 596.

question: Can a taxpayer with no qualifying children claim the earned income credit?
ground_truth: Yes. A taxpayer without a qualifying child can claim the earned income credit if they meet the basic eligibility rules, including having earned income, a valid Social Security number, and being between ages 25 and 64. The maximum credit for taxpayers with no qualifying children is $632 for 2024. See IRS Publication 596.

question: What is the maximum amount of investment income a taxpayer can have and still qualify for the earned income credit in 2024?
ground_truth: For 2024, a taxpayer's investment income cannot exceed $11,600 to be eligible for the earned income credit. Investment income includes interest, dividends, capital gain net income, and passive rental or royalty income. Exceeding this limit disqualifies the taxpayer regardless of earned income. See IRS Publication 596.

question: Can I deduct points paid when I took out a mortgage to purchase my main home?
ground_truth: Yes. Points paid to obtain a mortgage on your main home are generally fully deductible in the year paid, provided the loan is secured by your main home, charging points is an established business practice in your area, and the amount does not exceed the points generally charged locally. Points paid to refinance a mortgage must be deducted over the life of the loan. See IRS Publication 936.

question: Can I deduct interest on a home equity loan used to pay off credit card debt?
ground_truth: Generally no. Under the rules in effect through 2025, interest on home equity debt is deductible only if the loan proceeds are used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home that secures the loan. Interest on a home equity loan used to pay personal expenses such as credit card balances is not deductible. See IRS Publication 936.

question: What is the maximum American Opportunity Tax Credit per eligible student?
ground_truth: The maximum American Opportunity Tax Credit is $2,500 per eligible student per year. The credit equals 100% of the first $2,000 of qualified education expenses plus 25% of the next $2,000. Up to 40% of the credit (up to $1,000) is refundable. The credit is available only for the first four years of post-secondary education. See IRS Publication 970.

question: What is the modified AGI phase-out range for the American Opportunity Tax Credit for single filers?
ground_truth: The American Opportunity Tax Credit phases out for single filers with modified adjusted gross income between $80,000 and $90,000. For married filing jointly, the phase-out range is $160,000 to $180,000. No credit is available above these income limits. See IRS Publication 970.

question: How much student loan interest can I deduct per year?
ground_truth: You can deduct up to $2,500 of student loan interest paid per year. For 2024, the deduction phases out for single filers with modified AGI between $75,000 and $90,000, and for married filing jointly between $155,000 and $185,000. The deduction is an adjustment to income, so you do not need to itemize to claim it. See IRS Publication 970.

question: What is the Lifetime Learning Credit and how does it differ from the American Opportunity Credit?
ground_truth: The Lifetime Learning Credit is 20% of up to $10,000 of qualified education expenses, for a maximum credit of $2,000 per tax return. Unlike the American Opportunity Credit, it is not limited to the first four years of education, applies to graduate and professional courses, and is not refundable. The income phase-out range for single filers is $80,000 to $90,000 MAGI. See IRS Publication 970.

question: Are prizes and contest winnings taxable income?
ground_truth: Yes. Prizes and awards are generally included in gross income and are fully taxable at their fair market value, whether received as cash, merchandise, or trips. You must report the fair market value as income. An exception applies to certain employee achievement awards given as tangible personal property within dollar limits. See IRS Publication 525.
